summary:
The Latin American Wars of Independence were a series of revolutionary conflicts spanning from 1808 to 1833 that resulted in the independence of virtually all of Spain's and Portugal's colonies in the Americas. Together these movements fundamentally reshaped the political geography of the Western Hemisphere, transforming Spanish and Portuguese colonial possessions into more than a dozen independent republics and one empire.

The wars were triggered primarily by the crisis in the Iberian Peninsula that accompanied the [[event:109|Napoleonic Wars]]. When Napoleon invaded Spain in 1808 and forced the abdication of the Bourbon monarchy, placing his brother Joseph on the throne, the legitimacy of colonial authority was thrown into question. Creole elites — those of European descent born in the Americas — who had long chafed under trade restrictions and limited political participation saw an opportunity to assert greater autonomy, initially in the name of the deposed Spanish king and eventually for outright independence.

[[event:2527|Simón Bolívar]] became the dominant figure of South American independence, leading campaigns across present-day Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia. His visionary if ultimately unrealized goal was a unified Spanish American confederation. In the south, José de San Martín led independence movements in Argentina, Chile, and Peru. The two great liberators met at Guayaquil in 1822 in a famous private conference whose contents remain historically debated, after which San Martín withdrew from the struggle, leaving Bolívar to complete the liberation of South America.

[[event:2528|Mexican independence]] followed a distinctive path, beginning with the populist uprisings of Father Miguel Hidalgo and Father José María Morelos and culminating in a conservative-led independence under Agustín de Iturbide, who briefly ruled as Emperor Agustín I before a republic was established. Central America achieved independence in 1821, initially as part of the Mexican Empire before forming the short-lived Federal Republic of Central America.

[[event:2529|Brazil's path to independence]] was unique in the hemisphere. When the Portuguese royal family fled to Brazil in 1808 to escape Napoleon's invasion, it elevated the colony's status dramatically. Independence in 1822 came not through revolution but through the declaration of the Portuguese heir apparent, Prince Pedro, who proclaimed himself Emperor Pedro I of Brazil, creating a constitutional monarchy that persisted until 1889.

The wars' legacy was complex and contested. Independence brought formal political sovereignty but often left the deep social structures of colonial Latin America — racial hierarchy, land concentration, economic dependence — largely intact. The criollo elites who led most independence movements did not fundamentally challenge the subordination of indigenous peoples and those of African descent. Political instability, civil wars, and military coups plagued the newly independent states throughout the nineteenth century, as the difficult work of building viable republics from colonial institutions proved enormously challenging.
